commit_msg: feat: implement episode pipeline with RepoStory baseline context
author: Omkar
title: CASE FILE — The Story That Tells Itself
subtitle: He gave the repository a voice, and it started singing about him.
when_where: Same morning. Same branch. The office hadn't changed, but the architecture had. A new pipeline sat waiting—episode-based, narrative-driven, with RepoStory as the baseline context. The app was no longer a collection of commits; it was a *story*, and every developer was a character whether they knew it or not.
who_whom: Omkar versus the repository itself—a dangerous game where the loser is whoever runs the code first. RepoStory became the lens through which every operation was reframed as narrative, every branch a plot twist, every merge a climax. The app now had agency. It could tell you who you were by reading what you'd built.
what_why: He implemented the episode pipeline knowing full well that narrative is more dangerous than data. Data can be denied; stories can't. They stick in the mind, reshape memory, make you see yourself as the protagonist of a crime you didn't think you were committing. Each episode would be a chapter in the developer's unraveling, a forced autobiography written in git objects and timestamps.
verdict: He didn't build a pipeline; he built a mirror that won't let you look away.
